ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for giant river prawn vermicelli. FOODON_03311737 (prawn dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for giant river prawn vermicelli as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific composite seafood noodle dish.

main_ingredients: vermicelli noodles (glass noodles or rice vermicelli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Giant Scampi River Prawn Vermicelli is approximately 400 g, comprising one or two whole giant river prawns (approximately 200–250 g shell-on) served over a bed of vermicelli noodles with sauce and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked freshwater prawn (Macrobrachium rosenbergii) and glass noodle composite — estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries: Crustaceans, freshwater prawn, cooked (FDC ID 175180) and Glass noodles, cooked (FDC ID 169726), weighted 55% prawn flesh and 45% noodle with sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for giant river prawn vermicelli as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central data for cooked freshwater prawn (FDC ID 175180, approximately 55% of edible portion by weight) and cooked glass noodles (FDC ID 169726, approximately 45% of edible portion by weight), with a moderate sodium uplift to account for soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of this preparation. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glass noodles and vermicelli — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for giant river prawn vermicelli as a composite dish. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the GI of glass noodles / mung bean vermicelli (GI approximately 26–39, mean ~33) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The prawn component contributes negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is anchored to the noodle component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 54.0) / 100 = 18.9, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 13.5 × 4 = 54.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish in its standard restaurant preparation due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 1680 mg per serving (420 mg per 100 g × 400 g), which represents 73–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-the-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from vermicelli noodle component despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in moderation, as the glass noodle or rice vermicelli base has a low glycemic index (approximately 35), but the total glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate (approximately 18.9 GL units); portion control of the noodle component and pairing with the high-protein prawn content will help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol from prawn and high sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that giant river prawns contribute significant dietary cholesterol (approximately 110 mg per 100 g) and the dish carries a high sodium load from soy and oyster sauces; however, the low saturated fat content (approximately 0.4 g per 100 g) and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from the prawn are partially mitigating factors, and occasional moderate consumption may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Giant Scampi River Prawn Vermicelli is a premium Thai-influenced seafood noodle dish featuring one or two whole giant freshwater prawns (Macrobrachium rosenbergii), also known as giant river prawns or scampi, served over a bed of glass noodles or rice vermicelli. The dish is typically prepared by steaming or baking the prawns directly on top of the noodles, which absorb the prawn juices and a savoury sauce made from garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The prawns are prized for their sweet, firm, and succulent flesh, and their large size — often 150–250 g per prawn — makes this a visually impressive and indulgent dish. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and is considered a premium offering, typically priced at SGD 35–45 per portion. Nutritionally, the dish provides a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate profile: approximately 10.8 g protein and 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The noodle component contributes a low glycemic index (approximately 35), while the prawn contributes dietary cholesterol and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. Sodium content is elevated due to the seasoning sauces, making this a dish that individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should consume with caution. The dish is not suitable for those with crustacean, gluten, sesame, or mollusc allergies in its standard preparation.

giant river prawn: กุ้งแม่น้ำ

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for giant river prawn vermicelli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central data for cooked freshwater prawn and cooked glass noodles, with a sodium uplift for seasoning. GI is estimated from the closest analogous noodle type in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from USDA FoodData Central is high quality and the weighting rationale is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked freshwater prawn FDC ID 175180 and glass noodles FDC ID 169726) weighted at approximately 55% prawn flesh and 45% noodle with sauce. No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific composite dish. 2. Sodium is elevated (420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning; this is a modelled estimate and actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 3. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on glass noodle / mung bean vermicelli GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats in its standard preparation. 7. Halal status is marked false because oyster sauce (mollusc-derived) is a standard ingredient and the dish is not certified halal in its typical restaurant preparation. 8. The dish is classified as Thai cuisine as served in Singapore Thai seafood restaurants; the preparation style is Thai-Chinese fusion. 9. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant portion including shell-on prawn, noodles, and sauce; edible yield will be lower due to prawn shell weight.
